query: TCA cycle intermediates in hem synthesis

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Krebs Cycle -- Function -- Cataplerotic Processes
retrieved_docs.content: Citric acid intermediates can leave the cycle to participate in the biosynthesis of other compounds. Citrate can be directed toward fatty acids, succinyl-CoA to heme synthesis, alpha-ketoglutarate to amino acid synthesis, purine and neurotransmitter synthesis, oxaloacetate to amino acid synthesis, and malate to gluconeogenesis. [14] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Citric Acid Cycle -- Function -- Cataplerotic Processes
retrieved_docs.content: Citric acid intermediates may leave the cycle to biosynthesize other compounds. Citrate can be diverted to fatty acid synthesis; alpha-ketoglutarate to amino acid synthesis, neurotransmitter synthesis, and purine synthesis; succinyl-CoA to heme synthesis; malate to gluconeogenesis and oxaloacetate to amino acid synthesis. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Citric Acid Cycle -- Molecular Level -- Steps of the Citric Acid Cycle:
retrieved_docs.content: Citrate synthase catalyzes the condensation reaction of acetyl-CoA and oxaloacetate (the cycle’s final product) to form citrate, initiating the citric acid cycle. Note that this reaction is virtually irreversible with a delta-G-prime of -7.7 Kcal/M (thus strongly favoring citrate formation). Substrate and product availability regulate citrate synthase while citrate inhibits the enzyme oxaloacetate’s binding to the enzyme increases its affinity for acetyl-CoA. One should note that citrate serves as an inhibitory substrate for phosphofructokinase-1 in glycolysis and an activating substrate for acetyl CoA carboxylase in fatty acid synthesis. This point highlights the interconnectivity of our metabolic cycles - in short, no pathway occurs in a vacuum. [2] Isomerization of citrate Aconitase, an enzyme with an iron-sulfur center facilitates the hydroxyl group migration that makes isocitrate out of citrate. [3] Oxidative decarboxylation of isocitrate

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Krebs Cycle -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle, also known as the Krebs or citric acid cycle, is an important cell's metabolic hub (see Figure . Krebs Cycle). It comprises 8 enzymes within the mitochondrial matrix except the outlier succinate dehydrogenase, which is related to the respiratory chain on the inner mitochondrial membrane. The cycle is a gateway for aerobic metabolism for molecules that can convert to an acetyl group or dicarboxylic acid. Regulation of the TCA cycle occurs at 3 distinct points, including the following enzymes: citrate synthase, isocitrate dehydrogenase, and alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase. The cycle also plays a role in replenishing precursors for the storage form of fuels such as amino acids and cholesterol. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Krebs Cycle -- Function -- Isomerization of Citrate
retrieved_docs.content: The enzyme aconitase catalyzes the reversible conversion of citrate to isocitrate, which contains an iron-sulfur center that facilitates the hydroxyl group migration. Cis-aconitate is the intermediate product of this reaction. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Citric Acid Cycle -- Molecular Level -- Steps of the Citric Acid Cycle:
retrieved_docs.content: Citrate synthesis

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Krebs Cycle -- Function -- Anaplerotic Processes
retrieved_docs.content: Intermediates are inserted into the TCA cycle to replace the cataplerotic processes and ensure the continuation of the cycle. For instance, pyruvate can enter the cycle throughout the body through pyruvate carboxylase, which inserts additional oxaloacetate into the cycle. This process causes the reaction to be pushed forward toward the already exergonic citrate synthase, as there is more oxaloacetate to participate in the reaction. The liver is another example, as it can produce alpha-ketoglutarate by oxidative deamination or transamination of glutamate. [15] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Urea Cycle -- Cellular Level -- Step 4
retrieved_docs.content: Argininosuccinate is converted into arginine via argininosuccinate lyase. This reaction also gives off fumarate, which is involved in the mitochondrial generation of NADH in the TCA cycle, as well as tyrosine catabolism. Argininosuccinate à arginine + fumarate

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Citric Acid Cycle -- Function -- Anaplerotic Processes
retrieved_docs.content: Intermediates can also be inserted into the citric acid cycle to replace cataplerotic processes and ensure the cycle continues. For example, throughout the whole body, pyruvate can enter the cycle by way of pyruvate carboxylase, thus inserting additional oxaloacetate into the cycle. This increase in oxaloacetate pushes the cycle forward towards the already exergonic citrate synthase reaction. The liver is a particular case in that it can produce alpha-ketoglutarate by transamination or oxidative deamination of glutamate. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Citric Acid Cycle -- Molecular Level -- Steps of the Citric Acid Cycle:
retrieved_docs.content: This is the first step of the citric acid cycle that produces a reduced coenzyme. Here, isocitrate dehydrogenase oxidizes isocitrate, releasing a carbon dioxide molecule and reduces NAD+ to NADH and a proton. The nature of the reaction (releasing a gas) makes it irreversible. Isocitrate dehydrogenase is allosterically regulated: ADP and calcium ions activate it while ATP and NADH inhibit its activity. [4] Oxidative decarboxylation of alpha-ketoglutarate by the alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase complex

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Citric Acid Cycle -- Molecular Level -- Steps of the Citric Acid Cycle:
retrieved_docs.content: Succinate thiokinase catalyzes the cleavage of succinyl CoA’s thioester bond. The division of this high energy bond is coupled with the phosphorylation of guanosine diphosphate (GDP) and therefore produces GTP in addition to succinate. This coupled reaction is an example of substrate-level phosphorylation, as seen in glycolysis. [6] Oxidation of succinate

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Krebs Cycle -- Function -- Oxidative Decarboxylation of Isocitrate
retrieved_docs.content: The oxidative decarboxylation of isocitrate to alpha-ketoglutarate becomes catalyzed by NAD+ -dependent isocitrate dehydrogenase, producing CO2, NADH, and a proton; this is the rate-limiting step of the TCA cycle. The first reduced coenzyme production in the cycle occurs at this reaction. The tendency of this reaction to produce gas makes it irreversible. ADP and calcium ions allosterically regulate isocitrate dehydrogenase by activating it, while ATP and NADH inhibit its activity. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Heme Synthesis -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Porphyrin synthesis is the process that produces heme. Heme synthesis occurs partly in the mitochondria and partly in the cytosol. The biosynthesis involves an eight-step enzymatic pathway. Heme biosynthesis starts in mitochondria with the condensation of succinyl Co-A from the citric acid cycle and an amino acid glycine. They combine to produce a key heme intermediate, 5'-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) in mitochondria catalyzed by the pyridoxal phosphate-requiring (vitamin B6) enzyme, aminolevulinic acid synthase (ALAS). [3] This reaction is the rate-limiting step in the pathway. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Citric Acid Cycle -- Molecular Level -- Steps of the Citric Acid Cycle:
retrieved_docs.content: Fumarase is the catalyst in the hydration of fumarate to malate. [8] This reaction is reversible. In another attempt to highlight the interconnectedness of metabolic pathways, note that the urea cycle also produces fumarate. Oxidation of Malate

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Citric Acid Cycle -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The citric acid cycle serves as the mitochondrial hub for the final steps in carbon skeleton oxidative catabolism for carbohydrates, amino acids, and fatty acids. Each oxidative step, in turn, reduces a coenzyme such as nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NADH) or flavin adenine dinucleotide (FADH2). These reduced coenzymes contribute directly to the electron transport chain and thus to the majority of ATP production in the human body.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Citric Acid Cycle -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: After the PDC synthesizes acetyl-CoA, it enters the metabolic process known as the citric acid cycle (or the tricarboxylic acid cycle). This cycle has eight steps, seven of which are within the mitochondrial matrix and the outlier, succinate dehydrogenase is associated with the electron transport chain on the inner mitochondrial membrane. As stated above, this cycle results in the final oxidative steps of acetyl groups, resulting in the release of two molecules of carbon dioxide gas. The citric acid cycle further yields reduced coenzymes with each oxidative step; these coenzymes include NADH, GTP, and FADH2. The details of these redox reactions are in the Molecular subsection, as the discussion of these reactions should take place at the molecular level for best comprehension.

Finally, trisaccharides, as short carbohydrate chains, can serve as substrates or regulatory sugars that modulate glycosylation and other carbohydrate‑based biosynthetic processes. Together, these entities illustrate the wide range of chemical, pharmacologic, and biological factors that can define, regulate, or modify biosynthetic mechanisms, underscoring their importance for understanding metabolic regulation and for developing therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1895
retrieved_docs.content: S‑adenosylmethionine (S‑adenosylmethionin) and glutamine are both classified as members of the broader chemical group “amino acids and derivatives,” indicating that they share the fundamental structural features of amino acids and related compounds. This categorization reflects their roles in metabolism and biochemistry, as both participate in key physiological pathways typical of amino‑acid‑derived molecules. Recognizing S‑adenosylmethionine and glutamine as amino‑acid derivatives helps clinicians and researchers understand their functions, interactions, and potential therapeutic relevance within the context of amino‑acid metabolism.

Recognizing these relationships clarifies how antimetabolite and immunosuppressive agents are organized, their mechanistic targets, biosynthetic requirements, and potential for resistance, providing essential insight for patient management, metabolic research, and therapeutic development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1409
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium citrate is a chemical compound that is derived from citric acid, with citric acid serving as its parent molecule.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 566
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes are biological catalysts that are organized into several major subclasses, including isomerases, ligases, lyases, oxidoreductases, and transferases. Isomerases catalyze intramolecular rearrangements of chemical structures, ligases join two substrates together with the concomitant hydrolysis of a nucleoside triphosphate, lyases cleave bonds by means other than hydrolysis or oxidation, oxidoreductases mediate oxidation‑reduction reactions, and transferases move functional groups from one molecule to another. Beyond their essential metabolic functions, enzymes also play a central role in pharmacological effects, serving as the molecular mechanisms through which many drugs act, modulate physiological pathways, and influence therapeutic outcomes. Recognizing the classification of enzymes and their involvement in drug mechanisms is crucial for effective drug development and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1934
retrieved_docs.content: Nemoc is associated with اضطراب استقلاب الأحماض الأمينية, an amino‑acid metabolism disorder, indicating that individuals with nemoc may exhibit disturbances in amino‑acid metabolic pathways, which is relevant for diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1737
retrieved_docs.content: Oxid uhelnatý (carbon monoxide) is a central molecule that serves as the parent entity for the class anorganické sloučeniny uhlíku (inorganic carbon compounds), indicating that these compounds are derived from or related to carbon monoxide. At the same time, oxid uhelnatý is classified as an antimetabolite, reflecting its capacity to interfere with normal metabolic processes, and it is also recognized as a gasotransmitter, a gaseous signaling molecule that modulates physiological functions. By linking the categories of inorganic carbon chemistry, metabolic inhibition, and gaseous cellular communication, oxid uhelnatý illustrates a multifaceted role that is relevant for both biochemical understanding and potential therapeutic applications.

The integrated composition is intended for patients with iron‑deficiency anemia, folate deficiency, or increased nutritional needs, providing a convenient single‑dose regimen that addresses several micronutrient deficiencies simultaneously.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 5 -- Community 2929
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymology, the scientific study of enzymes and their biochemical actions, is recognized as a relevant qualifier for several distinct medical topics. In the context of Cannabis, enzymology helps elucidate how the plant’s cannabinoids are metabolized, how enzyme‑mediated pathways influence its pharmacodynamics, and how enzymatic interactions may affect both therapeutic benefits and adverse effects. Enzymology also provides essential insight into فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الحديد (iron‑deficiency anemia), where enzyme‑dependent processes such as heme synthesis, iron transport, and erythropoiesis are central to the disorder’s pathophysiology and to the development of targeted diagnostic and therapeutic strategies. Additionally, enzymology is pertinent to gout and other crystal arthropathies, because enzyme‑driven metabolic pathways—particularly those governing purine turnover, uric acid production, and crystal formation—underlie the onset and progression of these conditions. By integrating enzymological knowledge across Cannabis use, iron‑deficiency anemia, and gout‑related crystal arthropathies, clinicians and researchers can achieve a more comprehensive understanding of disease mechanisms, improve diagnostic accuracy, and tailor interventions that consider the underlying enzymatic processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2261
retrieved_docs.content: Carbon monoxide, referred to in Czech as oxid uhelnatý, is classified both as a gasotransmitter and as an antimetabolite. As a gasotransmitter, it functions as a small, endogenously produced gaseous signaling molecule that can modulate vascular tone, neurotransmission, and inflammatory responses. Its antimetabolite properties allow it to interfere with normal metabolic pathways, for example by binding to heme‑containing enzymes and altering cellular respiration. Exposure to carbon monoxide is directly associated with carbon‑monoxide poisoning (التسمم بأول أكسيد الكربون), a clinical syndrome characterized by hypoxic injury, neurological deficits, and potentially fatal outcomes. Recognizing carbon monoxide’s dual role as a physiological messenger and a toxic agent is essential for accurate diagnosis, prevention, and management of poisoning cases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 423
retrieved_docs.content: Acid‑base markers and blood gases are clinical parameters that reflect the status of key gaseous molecules in the circulatory system—carbon monoxide (CO), oxygen (O₂), and carbon dioxide (CO₂). CO, oxygen, and CO₂ belong to the broader families of oxides, gases, and oxygen‑containing compounds; CO and CO₂ are also classified as carbon‑containing products and inorganic carbon compounds, linking them to the radioactive isotope carbon‑14. CO functions as a gasotransmitter, an inverse subclass of signaling molecules, and is the causative agent in carbon monoxide poisoning. O₂ is the therapeutic agent used to treat carbon monoxide poisoning, highlighting the clinical interplay among these gases. CO₂ is specifically identified as an oxygen compound, reinforcing its classification within the larger group of oxygen compounds. This integrated view underscores the physiological, toxicological, and diagnostic relevance of carbon‑based gases, their inorganic forms, and related biomarkers in patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 284
retrieved_docs.content: Acid‑base markers and blood gases are classified as subcategories of the respiratory gases oxygen and carbon dioxide, reflecting their direct relationship to the measurement and regulation of these two key gaseous components in the bloodstream. Inorganic carbon compounds are likewise grouped under carbon dioxide, indicating that carbon‑containing inorganic substances are conceptually linked to this oxidized form of carbon. Carbon monoxide is identified as both a member of the broader class of gases and as an oxygen‑containing compound, underscoring its chemical nature as a gaseous oxide of carbon. Clinically, carbon monoxide is associated with carbon monoxide poisoning, a condition that is effectively treated with supplemental oxygen. Oxygen itself is a fundamental gas within the respiratory system and, together with carbon dioxide, forms the primary gaseous substrates examined by acid‑base and blood‑gas analyses. This network of relationships highlights how gases such as oxygen, carbon dioxide, and carbon monoxide, along with their related compound categories, are interwoven in both biochemical classification and patient‑care contexts.

The therapeutic rationale for pairing folic acid with iron is their synergistic support of erythropoiesis: folic acid is essential for DNA synthesis and red‑cell maturation, and its deficiency causes macrocytic anemia; iron is required for hemoglobin

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2737
retrieved_docs.content: Inorganic carbon is a chemical category that includes carbon monoxide (oxid uhelnatý) as a representative member, indicating that carbon monoxide is a specific form of inorganic carbon. Moreover, carbon monoxide is classified as a gasotransmitter, meaning it functions as a gaseous signaling molecule in physiological processes. Consequently, inorganic carbon, oxid uhelnatý, and gasotransmitery are interconnected, with carbon monoxide linking the broader class of inorganic carbon compounds to the functional group of gasotransmitters.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1902
retrieved_docs.content: L‑karnitin, S‑adenosylmethionin, and glutamin are all classified as members of the broader category “Amino acids and derivatives.” This grouping includes both standard amino acids and related compounds that serve essential roles in metabolism, such as energy transport (L‑karnitin), methyl group donation (S‑adenosylmethionin), and nitrogen balance and protein synthesis (glutamin). Recognizing that these three substances belong to the Amino acids and derivatives class helps clinicians and researchers appreciate their shared biochemical context and informs their use in therapeutic and nutritional strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1341
retrieved_docs.content: Beta‑D‑glucopyranose is classified as a saccharide, belonging to the broad group of carbohydrate molecules known as saccharidy. As a specific sugar monomer, beta‑D‑glucopyranose exemplifies the structural and functional characteristics of saccharides, which serve as essential energy sources, metabolic intermediates, and structural components in biological systems. Recognizing beta‑D‑glucopyranose as a member of the saccharidy class aids in understanding its role in nutrition, biochemical pathways, and laboratory analyses of carbohydrate content.
